elements.sent-2411-001: Jiang et al. \institute{National Engineering Research Center of Speech and Language Information Processing, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, P.. R.}
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper proposes ESTVocoder, a novel excitation-spectral-transformed neural vocoder within the framework of source-filter theory.
elements.sent-2411-004: The ESTVocoder transforms the amplitude and phase spectra of the excitation into the corresponding speech amplitude and phase spectra using a neural filter whose backbone is ConvNeXt v2 blocks.
elements.sent-2411-005: Finally, the speech waveform is reconstructed through the inverse short-time Fourier transform (ISTFT).
elements.sent-2411-006: The excitation is constructed based on the F0: for voiced segments, it contains full harmonic information, while for unvoiced segments, it is represented by noise.
elements.sent-2411-007: The excitation provides the filter with prior knowledge of the amplitude and phase patterns, expecting to reduce the modeling difficulty compared to conventional neural vocoders.
elements.sent-2411-008: To ensure the fidelity of the synthesized speech, an adversarial training strategy is applied to ESTVocoder with multi-scale and multi-resolution discriminators.
elements.sent-2411-009: Analysis-synthesis and text-to-speech experiments both confirm that our proposed ESTVocoder outperforms or is comparable to other baseline neural vocoders, e.g., HiFi-GAN, SiFi-GAN, and Vocos, in terms of synthesized speech quality, with a reasonable model complexity and generation speed.
elements.sent-2411-010: Additional analysis experiments also demonstrate that the introduced excitation effectively accelerates the model's convergence process, thanks to the speech spectral prior information contained in the excitation.
elements.sent-2411-011: Text-to-speech (TTS) is a technique that utilizes machines to convert written text content into audible speech.
elements.sent-2411-012: With the recent advancements in deep learning, there has been a significant improvement in the clarity and naturalness of synthesized speech.
elements.sent-2411-013: A TTS system typically consists of two key components,i.e., an acoustic model and a vocoder.
elements.sent-2411-014: The acoustic model is responsible for predicting acoustic features (e.g., mel spectrogram) from the input text.
elements.sent-2411-015: Subsequently, the vocoder uses these features to generate the final speech waveform.
elements.sent-2411-016: Besides TTS, other fields such as voice conversion (VC) and singing voice synthesis (SVS) also require a vocoder to reconstruct waveform.
elements.sent-2411-017: Thus, a robust vocoder is crucial for the field of speech signal processing, which is also the focus of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-018: Traditional vocoders, such as STRAIGHT \cite{kawahara1999restructuring and WORLD \cite{morise2016world , synthesize speech waveform using traditional signal processing methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Although they are computationally simple and fast, result in synthesized speech with lower naturalness.
elements.sent-2411-020: With the development of deep learning, the WaveNet \cite{oord2016wavenet represents a significant milestone in synthesized speech quality.}
elements.sent-2411-021: WaveNet is an autoregressive neural vocoder capable of producing natural and clear speech waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-022: Unlike traditional vocoders that rely on traditional signal processing methods, WaveNet relies entirely on end-to-end neural network training.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, autoregressive neural vocoders generate audio samples sequentially and use previously generated samples to create new ones, leading to extremely low efficiency and high computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-024: To address the issue of low efficiency in autoregressive models, researchers have proposed various alternative approaches, including knowledge distillation-based models \cite{oord2018parallel,ping2018clarinet , flow-based models \cite{prenger2019waveglow,ping2020waveflow , and glottis-based models \cite{juvela2019glotnet,valin2019lpcnet .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Although these models have improved inference efficiency, their overall computational complexity remains high.
elements.sent-2411-026: To overcome the aforementioned issues, non-autoregressive neural vocoders have gradually been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-027: Non-autoregressive models generate all samples in parallel, offering high computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-028: For instance, HiFi-GAN \cite{kong2020hifi vocoder maintains high naturalness in synthesized audio thanks to the generative adversarial network (GAN) \cite{goodfellow2014generative based training while balancing high generation speed.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, there is still room for efficiency improvement with these vocoders, as they directly predict high-temporal-resolution waveforms from input acoustic features.
elements.sent-2411-030: The substantial discrepancy in time resolution between the acoustic fratures and waveforms, results in extensive upsampling operations on the acoustic features, leading to significant computational demands.
elements.sent-2411-031: Thus, subsequent neural vocoders have adopted the approach of predicting amplitude and phase spectrum and then reconstructing the waveform using the inverse short-time Fourier transform (ISTFT).
elements.sent-2411-032: For instance, Vocos \cite{siuzdak2023vocos with ConvNeXt blocks \cite{liu2022convnet as backbone, directly predicts the amplitude and phase spectrum at the same temporal resolution from the input acoustic features, thereby maintaining the same feature resolution at all frame rates.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Vocos has increased its generation speed by more than tenfold compared to HiFi-GAN while maintaining high-quality synthesized speech.
elements.sent-2411-034: Most of the aforementioned vocoders only use the mel spectrogram as input, which is convenient, but the mel spectrogram is a compressed representation of the amplitude spectrum and may lose some acoustic details.
elements.sent-2411-035: Therefore, many vocoders that utilize other acoustic features have also been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-036: A common approach is to enhance vocoder performance based on the source-filter theory framework by introducing the F0 as an additional acoustic feature.
elements.sent-2411-037: Neural source filter (NSF) model \cite{wang2019neural is a pioneer in applying neural networks within the source-filter framework, which synthesizes speech waveform directly based on explicit F0 and mel spectrograms.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Recently, some works combining source-filter vocoders with GAN-based training, such as SiFi-GAN \cite{yoneyama2023source and SF-GAN \cite{lu2022source , have been proposed.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: This type of vocoders generates excitation based on the F0.
elements.sent-2411-040: The excitation waveform is then processed through a neural filter conditioned on the mel spectrogram to directly produce the final speech waveform.
elements.sent-2411-041: Experiments have shown that after introducing the additional F0 features, the quality of the speech generated by these vocoders is obviously improved.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, their excitations are often constructed based on single F0, lacking harmonic information, which may impact the reconstruction performance of the neural filter.
elements.sent-2411-043: In addition, these methods often rely on direct transformation of the excitation waveform, still leaving room for improvements in efficiency and model complexity.
elements.sent-2411-044: Excitation-spectral-transformed methods in source-fileter-based neural vocoders have not yet been thoroughly investigated.
elements.sent-2411-045: To achieve high-fidelity speech synthesis as well as efficient training and rapid generation speed, we propose a novel excitation-spectral-transformed neural vocoder called ESTVocoder.
elements.sent-2411-046: The ESTVocoder is designed based on the source-filter theory and first produces an excitation according to the F0.
elements.sent-2411-047: Compared to the single-F0-based excitation used by SiFi-GAN \cite{yoneyama2023source and SF-GAN \cite{lu2022source , the proposed ESTVocoder utilizes a full-harmonic excitation which contains richer spectral information.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Subsequently, a neural filter with ConvNeXt v2 blocks \cite{woo2023convnext as the backbone transforms the amplitude and phase spectra of the excitation into the corresponding amplitude and phase spectra of the speech, conditioned on the mel spectrogram.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Finally, the speech waveform is reconstructed via ISTFT.
elements.sent-2411-050: Both analysis-synthesis and TTS experimental results show that our proposed ESTVocoder outperforms or is comparable to HiFi-GAN, SiFi-GAN, and Vocos, in terms of synthesized speech quality.
elements.sent-2411-051: Our proposed ESTVocoder also has an extremely fast training convergence speed, thanks to the introduction of spectral prior information contained in the excitation.
elements.sent-2411-052: This paper is organized as follows: In Section \ref{sec:pagestyle , we provide details on our proposed ESTVocoder.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In Section \ref{sec:exp , we present our experimental results.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Finally, we give conclusions in Section \ref{sec:con .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{estvocoder_overview.pdf} \caption{Overview of the proposed ESTVocoder. Here, CAT represents the concatenation operation.} \label{Overview}
elements.sent-2411-055: Fig. \ref{Overview shows an overview of the proposed ESTVocoder model.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In ESTVocoder, the F0 sequence $\bm{f}\in\mathbb{R ^F$ first passes through an excitation producer to produce an excitation waveform $\bm{e}\in\mathbb{R ^T$, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{equ: e_producer} e=EP(f,S_r),\end{equation} where $EP$ represents the excitation producer and $S_r$ is waveform sampling rate.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Assuming the frame shift for extracting F0 and spectral features from the waveform is $w_s$, it holds $T=F\cdot w_s$.
elements.sent-2411-058: Then, a spectral-transformed neural filter transforms the amplitude spectrum $\bm{A _e\in\mathbb{R ^{F\times N}$ and phase spectrum $\bm{P _e\in\mathbb{R ^{F\times N}$ extracted from the excitation waveform $\bm{e}$ by STFT into the corresponding speech amplitude spectrum $\hat{\bm{A }\in\mathbb{R ^{F\times N}$ and phase spectrum $\hat{\bm{P }\in\mathbb{R ^{F\times N}$, with the mel spectrogram $\bm{M}\in\mathbb{R ^{F\times M}$ as conditions, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{equ: e} A_e,P_e=STFT(e),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{equ: S} \bm{\hat{A}},\bm{\hat{P}}=ST-NF(A_e,P_e \vert M),\end{equation} where $ST\text{- NF$ denotes the spectral-transformed neural filter.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Finally, the predicted speech amplitude and phase spectra are reconstructed into the speech waveform $\hat{\bm{x }\in\mathbb{R ^{T}$ through the ISTFT, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{equ: ISTFT} \hat{x}=ISTFT(\bm{\hat{A}} \cdot exp(j\bm{\hat{P}})).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The excitation producer generates the corresponding excitation waveform $\bm{e}$ based on the frame-level F0 sequence $\bm{f}$.
elements.sent-2411-061: First, a point-level F0 sequence $\bm{f _{pl = \left[f_1, \ldots, f_T\right]^\top$ is first generated by repeating the F0 value of $\bm{f}$ within each frame, which then serves as the input to the excitation producer.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The excitation producer outputs an excitation signal $\bm{e =\left[e_1, \ldots, e_T\right]^\top$, which is a sine-based signal at voiced segments and Gaussian white noise at unvoiced segments.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Specifically, the excitation waveform can be represented as: \begin{equation}\label{eq1} e_t=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\sum\limits_{k=1}\limits^K\alpha \sin(\sum\limits_{h=1}\limits^t 2\pi k\frac{f_h}{S_r} ) + n_t, f_t > 0,t\in V_j \frac{1}{3\sigma}n_t, f_t=0\end{array}\right.,\end{equation} where $K = \left\lfloor \frac{S_r/2 {\text{min (\bm{f ) \right\rfloor$ represents the minimum number of harmonics required to cover the entire frequency band.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The left subplot of Fig. \ref{analysis1 gives a visualization example of the amplitude spectrum of the excitation waveform.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Compared to previous works \cite{lu2022source,ai2020neural , the most significant difference in our proposed excitation is that our excitation includes all possible harmonic information.}
elements.sent-2411-066: In this way, the produced excitation already includes the basic acoustic pattern of the speech waveform, expecting to effectively alleviate the modeling and learning difficulty for the subsequent neural filter.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{e_estvocoder2.pdf} \caption{Visualization of the amplitude spectrum of the excitation waveform and the speech waveform generated by ESTVocoder. } \label{analysis1}
elements.sent-2411-067: Unlike the waveform-transformed neural filters used in other vocoders, the neural filter in ESTVocoder transforms the amplitude spectrum $\bm{A _e$ and phase spectrum $\bm{P _e$ of the excitation waveform $\bm{e}$ into the speech amplitude spectrum $\hat{\bm{A }$ and phase spectrum $\hat{\bm{P }$ conditioned on the mel spectrogram $\bm{M}$, and then reconstructs the speech waveform $\hat{\bm{x }$ through ISTFT.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Fig. \ref{analysis1 provides an visualization example of amplitude spectral transformation (from the left subplot to the right subplot).}
elements.sent-2411-069: Therefore, the role of the neural filter is to achieve the gradual spectral refinement using a neural network, guided by the information from the mel spectrogram.
elements.sent-2411-070: As mentioned in Section \ref{sec:excit , the excitation spectrum already has the rudiments of the speech spectrum, essentially providing the neural filter with prior spectral information.}
elements.sent-2411-071: We expect that such an excitation can reduce the learning difficulty of the neural filter model, thereby accelerating the model's convergence speed, while also diminishing the smooth artificial artifacts in the amplitude and phase spectra of the synthesized speech.
elements.sent-2411-072: Specifically, the amplitude and phase spectra of the excitation are concatenated along the dimensional axis and then passed through a dimension-reduction layer.
elements.sent-2411-073: The mel-spectrogram, used as a condition, is first injected into a dimension-expansion layer to match the dimension of the dimension-reduced amplitude-phase feature.
elements.sent-2411-074: Then, these two features with same dimension are added together and passed through the backbone network (i.e., ConvNeXt v2) along with a layer normalization layer and a feed-forward layer to predict the speech amplitude and phase spectra.
elements.sent-2411-075: Inspired by Vocos \cite{siuzdak2023vocos , we split the output of the above feed-forward layer into two features along the dimensional axis.}
elements.sent-2411-076: One feature generates the amplitude spectrum through exponential activation, while the other feature generates the phase spectrum by first applying sine/cosine calculations and then the atan2 function.
elements.sent-2411-077: The neural filter adopts a ConvNeXt v2 \cite{woo2023convnext as its backbone, which has been proven to possess superior modeling capabilities compared to the commonly used ResNet networks \cite{he2016deep in HiFi-GAN \cite{kong2020hifi and the ConvNeXt network \cite{liu2022convnet in Vocos \cite{siuzdak2023vocos .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The backbone ConvNeXt v2 network consists of multiple cascaded identical ConvNeXt v2 blocks.
elements.sent-2411-079: As shown in Fig. \ref{ConvNeXtv2 , the key components of the ConvNeXt v2 block include a 1D depth convolutional layer, a layer normalization layer, a feed-forward layer that projects features to higher dimensions, a Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) activation \cite{hendrycks2016gaussian , a global response normalization (GRN) layer \cite{woo2023convnext , and an another feed-forward layer that projects features back to their original dimensions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The GRN layer comprises three integral steps, i.e., aggregating global features, normalizing these features, and calibrating them, thereby enhancing feature diversity and ultimately boosting the representational quality.
elements.sent-2411-081: The final output of the ConvNeXt v2 block is obtained by adding the input of the 1D depth convolutional layer and the output of the last feed-forward layer (i.e., residual connection).
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{ConvNeXt_v2.pdf} \caption{The specific compositional structure of the ConvNeXt v2 block.} \label{ConvNeXtv2}
elements.sent-2411-082: In ESTVocoder, only the neural network filter contains trainable parameters, and it is trained using an adversarial training approach.
elements.sent-2411-083: The ESTVocoder uses multi-period discriminator (MPD) \cite{kong2020hifi and multi-resolution discriminator (MRD) \cite{jang2021univnet for adversarial training to ensure the quality of synthesized waveforms.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Each sub-MPD converts the input synthesized waveform $\hat{\bm{x }$ or natural waveform $\bm{x}$ into a 2D periodic map based on the preset period.}
elements.sent-2411-085: This mapping result then goes through a series of 5 consecutive processing stages, each stage containing a layer of 2D convolution and a leaky rectified linear unit (LReLU) activation function.
elements.sent-2411-086: The final output of the processing chain is further processed through a 2D convolutional output layer to generate discriminative scores.
elements.sent-2411-087: The period parameters are set to 2, 3, 5, 7, and 11, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-088: Each sub-MRD uses the amplitude spectrum extracted from $\hat{\bm{x }$ or $\bm{x}$ as input, based on specific STFT parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Assuming the STFT parameters for extracting the input amplitude and phase spectrum for the neural filter are: [frame length, frame shift, FFT point number] = [$w_l$, $w_s$, $2N+1$].
elements.sent-2411-090: We set the STFT parameters for the three sub-MRDs as [$w_l/2$, $w_s/2$, $(2N+1)/2$], [$w_l$, $w_s$, $2N+1$] and [$2w_l$, $2w_s$, $2(2N+1)$], respectively.
elements.sent-2411-091: Each sub-MRD consists of blocks made up of 2D convolutional layer and LReLU activation, followed by a 2D convolutional layer that provides the final output.
elements.sent-2411-092: We use the hinge form of adversarial loss.
elements.sent-2411-093: Assuming $D*$ represents the sub-discriminator of MPD or MRD, the corresponding generator adversarial loss and discriminator adversarial loss are as follows. \begin{equation}\label{equ: adv G} \mathcal L_{adv-G}^*=E_{\hat{x}}\max \left(0,1-D^*(\hat{x})\right),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{equ: adv D} \mathcal L_{adv-D}^*=E_{\left(\hat{x},x\right)}\left[\max \left(0,1-D^*(x)\right) + \max \left(0,1+D^*(\hat{x})\right)\right].\end{equation} Additionally, feature matching loss $\mathcal L_{FM ^*$ \cite{kumar2019melgan is also utilized, characterized by the summation of the mean absolute error (MAE) between the corresponding intermediate layer outputs of sub-discriminator $D^*$ when provided with inputs $\hat{\bm{x }$ or $\bm{x}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: In addition, we also incorporate the mean absolute error (MAE) loss on the mel spectrogram between the extracted mel spectrogram $\hat{\bm{M }$ and natural one $\bm{M}$ derived from synthesized waveform $\hat{\bm{x }$ and natural one $\bm{x}$, respectively, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{equ: M Loss} \mathcal L_{M}=\dfrac{1}{FM}\cdotE_{\left(\hat{M},M\right)}\left( \left\lVert \hat{M}-M\right\rVert_1\right).\end{equation} Therefore, the final GAN-based losses for generator (i.e., the neural filter) and discriminator (i.e., the MPD and MRD) are respectively defined by the following expressions. \begin{equation}\label{equ: GAN G} \mathcal L_{G}=\sum_{i=1}^5 \left(\mathcal L_{adv-G}^{Pi}+\mathcal L_{FM}^{Pi} \right)+\lambda_{MRD}\sum_{j=1}^3 \left(\mathcal L_{adv-G}^{Rj}+\mathcal L_{FM}^{Rj} \right)+\lambda_{M}\mathcal L_{M},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{equ: GAN D} \mathcal L_{D}=\sum_{i=1}^5 \mathcal L_{adv-D}^{Pi}+\lambda_{MRD}\sum_{j=1}^3 \mathcal L_{adv-D}^{Rj},\end{equation} where $Pi$ and $Rj$ represent $i$-th sub-MPD and $j$-th sub-MRD, $\lambda_{MRD}$ and $\lambda_{M}$ are hyperparameters.}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\label{equ: GAN G} \mathcal L_{G}=\sum_{i=1}^5 \left(\mathcal L_{adv-G}^{Pi}+\mathcal L_{FM}^{Pi} \right)+\lambda_{MRD}\sum_{j=1}^3 \left(\mathcal L_{adv-G}^{Rj}+\mathcal L_{FM}^{Rj} \right)+\lambda_{M}\mathcal L_{M},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-095: The training process follows the standard training mode of GAN, i.e., using $\mathcal L_G$ and $\mathcal L_{D}$ to train the generator and discriminator alternately.
elements.sent-2411-096: During the test stage, we apply the proposed ESTVocoder on two tasks, i.e., the analysis-synthesis task and TTS task.
elements.sent-2411-097: The process of analysis-synthesis applications is consistent with the training process.
elements.sent-2411-098: We first extract the natural F0 and the natural mel spectrogram from the natural speech and then inject them into the well-trained ESTVocoder to generate the speech waveform.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{f0predictor.pdf} \caption{The structure of the F0 predictor. Here, CAT and Sig. represents the concatenation operation and sigmoid activation.} \label{f0predictor}
elements.sent-2411-099: In TTS tasks, after predicting the mel spectrogram from text via an acoustic model, we use an F0 predictor \cite{lu2022source to predict the F0 from the mel spectrograms.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Then the predicted F0 and mel spectrogram are inject into the well-trained ESTVocoder to generate the speech waveform.
elements.sent-2411-101: The structure of the F0 predictor is shown in Fig. \ref{f0predictor .}
elements.sent-2411-102: The mel spectrogram is first input into three parallel convolutional layers with different kernel sizes to obtain three sets of intermediate features.
elements.sent-2411-103: Subsequently, the concatenated features are inputted into two parallel feed-forward layers with different activation functions (Sigmoid and ReLU, respectively) to separately derive the F0 contour and the voiced/unvoiced (V/UV) flag.
elements.sent-2411-104: Finally, multiply the F0 contour and the V/UV flag to obtain the final F0 sequence.
elements.sent-2411-105: We conducted experiments on the LJSpeech \cite{ito2017lj dataset, which included 13,100 speech utterances from an English female for a total of approximately 24 hours.}
elements.sent-2411-106: All speech waveforms were downsampled to 16 kHz for our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-107: We randomly selected 10,480 utterances for training, 1,310 for validation, and remaining 1,310 for testing.
elements.sent-2411-108: When performing the STFT, the frame length was 640 (i.e., $w_l=640$), the frame shift was 160 (i.e., $w_s=160$), and the number of FFT points was 1024 (i.e., $N=513$).
elements.sent-2411-109: In the neural filter model, before the ConvNeXt v2 block, both the layer dimension-reduction and dimension-expansion layers had 512 nodes.
elements.sent-2411-110: The number of ConvNeXt v2 blocks in the ConvNeXt v2 network was set to 8.
elements.sent-2411-111: For each ConvNeXt v2 block, the kernel size and channel size were set to 7 and 512, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-112: The model was trained on on a single NVIDIA RTX TITAN GPU and optimized using the AdamW optimizer with $\beta_1$ = 0.8, $\beta_2$ = 0.99, and weight decay of 0.01.
elements.sent-2411-113: The learning rate was set initially to $2\times10^{-4}$ and scheduled to decay with a factor of 0.999 at every epoch.
elements.sent-2411-115: SiFi-GAN introduces excitation into HiFi-GAN, constructing a neural vocoder within the source-filter framework.
elements.sent-2411-116: In contrast to HiFi-GAN and SiFi-GAN, Vocos models the amplitude and phase spectra instead of directly modeling the waveform.
elements.sent-2411-117: However, it is not designed within the source-filter framework.
elements.sent-2411-118: Therefore, comparing them with the proposed ESTVocoder is fair and reasonable.
elements.sent-2411-119: All the baseline models were trained on the same LJSpeech dataset with the same training environment as ESTVocoder.
elements.sent-2411-120: In this paper, we conducted analysis-synthesis experiments on HiFi-GAN, SiFi-GAN, Vocos, and ESTVocoder.
elements.sent-2411-121: We performed TTS experiments only on HiFi-GAN, Vocos, and ESTVocoder using mel spectrograms predicted by FastSpeech2 \cite{ren2020fastspeech \footnote{\href{https://github.com/ming024/FastSpeech2 {https://github.com/ming024/FastSpeech2 .. .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Since SiFi-GAN utilized mel cepstral features extracted by WORLD \cite{morise2016world and no TTS experiments were conducted in the original paper \cite{yoneyama2023source , we also discarded the TTS experiment on SiFi-GAN.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: For the analysis-synthesis experiment, we have adopted a variety of objective metrics to evaluate the quality of synthesized speech from multiple perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-124: To evaluate the amplitude-related quality, the root mean square error of logarithmic amplitude spectrum (LAS-RMSE) and mel cepstrum distortion (MCD) were used.
elements.sent-2411-125: To evaluate the F0 modeling accuracy, root mean square error of F0 (F0-RMSE) and V/UV flag error (denoted by V/UV) were adopted.
elements.sent-2411-126: Finally, to comprehensively evaluate the quality of synthesized speech, we introduced two commonly used tools, including perceptual evaluation of speech quality (PESQ) \cite{rec2007p and virtual speech quality objective listener (ViSQOL) \cite{chinen2020visqol .}}
elements.sent-2411-127: To evaluate the generation efficiency of vocoders, we compared the real-time factor (RTF) and training speed.
elements.sent-2411-128: The RTF is calculated as the ratio of generation time and the actual duration of the test set.
elements.sent-2411-129: The training speed is defined as the seconds taken to complete one epoch of training (s/e), which reflects the efficiency of model training.
elements.sent-2411-130: For the TTS experiment, we employed a subjective mean opinion score (MOS) test, to compare the naturalness of the vocoders.
elements.sent-2411-131: In each MOS test, 20 test utterances synthesized by compared vocoders along with the natural utterances were compared and evaluated by at least 30 native English listeners.
elements.sent-2411-132: This test was conducted on the Amazon Mechanical Turk crowd-sourcing platform, where listeners were asked to rate the naturalness on a scale from 1 to 5, with a score interval of 0.5.
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Quality-related objective evaluation results of for ESTVocoder, HiFi-GAN, SiFi-GAN and Vocos on the test set of the LJSpeech for the analysis-synthesis experiments. The bold and underline numbers indicate the optimal and sub-optimal results, respectively.}\label{tab1} \adjustbox{width=0.8\textwidth}{ \begin{tabular}{l c c c c c c} \multirow{2}{*} { {LAS-RMSE} } { {MCD} } {F0-RMSE} { {V/UV} } { PESQ$\uparrow$} { ViSQOL$\uparrow$} { { (dB)$\downarrow$}} { (dB)$\downarrow$} { { (cent)$\downarrow$}} { { (\%)$\downarrow$}} { { } } { { }} { ESTVocoder} 36.51 0.931 4.17 4.99 3.70 4.895 { HiFi-GAN} 40.34 1.227 5.07 5.23 3.48 4.815 { SiFi-GAN} 29.39 1.252 5.03 5.15 3.33 4.703 { Vocos} 39.07 1.033 4.40 5.31 3.51 4.863 \end{tabular}}
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Efficiency-related objective evaluation results of for ESTVocoder, HiFi-GAN, SiFi-GAN and Vocos on the test set of the LJSpeech for the analysis-synthesis experiments. The bold and underline numbers indicate the optimal and sub-optimal results, respectively. Here, s/e represents second per epoch and "$a\times$" represents $a\times$ real time.}\label{tab2} \adjustbox{width=0.8\textwidth}{ \begin{tabular}{l c c c} \multirow{2}{*} { RTF } { RTF } { Training Speed } { (GPU)$\downarrow$} { (CPU)$\downarrow$} { (s/e)$\downarrow$} { ESTVocoder} 0.0016 (625.0$\times$) 0.0112 (89.3$\times$) 203 { HiFi-GAN} 0.0013 (769.2$\times$) 0.1496 (6.8$\times$) 358 { SiFi-GAN} 0.0135 (74.1$\times$) 0.1568 (6.4$\times$) 567 { Vocos} 0.0009 (1111.1$\times$) 0.0093 (107.5$\times$) 199 \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-133: The quality-related objective evaluation results of the proposed ESTVocoder and baseline HiFi-GAN, SiFi-GAN and Vocos for the analysis-synthesis experiments are presented in Table \ref{tab1 .}
elements.sent-2411-134: Regarding the amplitude quality, it can be observed that our proposed ESTVocoder achieved the lowest MCD results, but it was second only to SiFi-GAN in the LAS-RMSE metric.
elements.sent-2411-135: However, the MCD calculation is performed in the mel domain, which better reflects human perception.
elements.sent-2411-136: Therefore, it can be inferred that the synthesized speech of our proposed ESTVocoder had higher perceived quality.
elements.sent-2411-137: For F0 recovery capability, our proposed ESTVocoder had the lowest F0-RMSE and V/UV error, indicating its strong F0 restoration capability and fewer pronunciation errors.
elements.sent-2411-138: Thanks to ESTVocoder's excellent performance in amplitude spectrum perceptual quality and fundamental frequency recovery capability, it is unsurprising that its overall speech quality is also the best, according to PESQ and ViSQOL results.
elements.sent-2411-139: Therefore, our proposed ESTVocoder outperformed commonly used waveform-prediction-based neural vocoders (i.e., HiFi-GAN), excitation-waveform-transformed neural vocoders (e.g., SiFi-GAN), and spectral-prediction-based neural vocoders (i.e., Vocos) in terms of synthesized speech quality.
elements.sent-2411-140: This confirms the advantages of our proposed excitation spectral transformation method.
elements.sent-2411-141: Table \ref{tab2 gives the results of the comparison of the generation and training speeds among the proposed ESTVocoder, HiFi-GAN, SiFi-GAN and Vocos for the analysis-synthesis experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-142: We first compared the proposed ESTVocoder with Vocos, as both were designed to predict spectra rather than waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-143: The generation and training efficiency of ESTVocoder were slightly lower than those of Vocos, which is reasonable given that ESTVocoder introduced an additional excitation producer module.
elements.sent-2411-144: This conclusion is consistent with the comparison between HiFi-GAN and SiFi-GAN.
elements.sent-2411-145: When compared with the two waveform-prediction-based neural vocoders, i.e., HiFi-GAN and SiFi-GAN, although our proposed ESTVocoder had a slightly slower generation speed on GPU compared to HiFi-GAN, its generation speed on CPU was significantly faster than both HiFi-GAN and SiFi-GAN.
elements.sent-2411-146: This demonstrates the efficiency advantage of predicting spectra rather than waveforms, especially when GPU parallel acceleration is not available.
elements.sent-2411-147: Additionally, the training efficiency of ESTVocoder is also significantly higher than that of HiFi-GAN and SiFi-GAN.
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Subjective evaluation results of ESTVocoder, HiFi-GAN and Vocos on the test set of the LJSpeech for the TTS experiments.}\label{tab1MOS} \adjustbox{width=0.3\textwidth}{ \begin{tabular}{l c} { {MOS$\uparrow$}} { Natural} 4.01±0.044 { ESTVocoder} 3.87±0.051 { HiFi-GAN} 3.90±0.047 { Vocos} 3.87±0.049 \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-148: For TTS experiments, the results of the subjective MOS test on the natural speech and speeches generated by ESTVocoder, HiFi-GAN and Vocos are shown in Table \ref{tab1MOS .}
elements.sent-2411-149: We can see that their average MOS was similar.
elements.sent-2411-150: To determine the significance of the differences among them, we also calculated the $p$-value of the $t$-test.
elements.sent-2411-151: The results of the two $t$-tests between ESTVocoder and HiFi-GAN and between ESTVocoder and Vocos indicated that the performance differences between the ESTVocoder and other baseline vocoders were not significant ($p$ > 0.05) in the TTS task.
elements.sent-2411-152: Therefore, our proposed ESTVocoder is comparable to other baseline vocoders in terms of subjective quality on TTS tasks.
elements.sent-2411-153: However, it achieves better objective results in analysis-synthesis tasks and has considerable training and generation speed advantages.
elements.sent-2411-154: In the following subsection, we also demonstrate that ESTVocoder has a faster training convergence speed, thus saving training costs, which is also one of its advantages.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{e-vocos_vocosv2-analysis4_00.pdf} \caption{Visualized amplitude spectra of natural speech and speeches generated by Vocos-CNXv2*, ESTVocoder*, Vocos-CNXv2 and ESTVocoder (example sentence LJ034-0143)} \label{analysis}
elements.sent-2411-155: Choosing a good backbone is crucial for the performance of the synthesized speech by a neural vocoder.
elements.sent-2411-156: As mentioned in Section \ref{ssec:block , we choose ConvNeXt v2 blocks as the backbone for our proposed ESTVocoder due to their proven strong modeling capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-157: However, Vocos uses ConvNeXt blocks as its backbone.
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Quality-related objective evaluation results of for ESTVocoder, ESTVocoder-CNXv1, Vocos-CNXv2 and Vocos on the test set of the LJSpeech for the analysis-synthesis experiments.}\label{tab4} \adjustbox{width=0.8\textwidth}{ \begin{tabular}{l ccccc c } \multirow{2}{*} { {LAS-RMSE} } { {MCD} } {F0-RMSE} { {V/UV} } { PESQ$\uparrow$} { ViSQOL$\uparrow$} { { (Hz)$\downarrow$}} { (dB)$\downarrow$} { { (dB)$\downarrow$}} { { (\%)$\downarrow$}} { { } } { { }} { ESTVocoder} 36.51 0.931 4.17 4.99 3.70 4.895 { ESTVocoder-CNXv1} 37.60 1.025 4.27 5.46 3.63 4.880 { Vocos-CNXv2} 38.53 0.998 4.30 5.30 3.58 4.878 { Vocos} 39.07 1.033 4.40 5.31 3.51 4.863 \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-158: Although researchers have systematically compared the differences between ConvNeXt and ConvNeXt v2 in the field of image processing \cite{woo2023convnext , this difference has yet to be systematically validated in the field of speech signal processing.}
elements.sent-2411-159: Therefore, we replaced all ConvNeXt v2 blocks in ESTVocoder with ConvNeXt blocks (referred to as ESTVocoder-CNXv1) and compared it with the original ESTVocoder.
elements.sent-2411-160: Simultaneously, we replaced all ConvNeXt blocks in Vocos with ConvNeXt v2 blocks (referred to as Vocos-CNXv2) and compared it with the original Vocos.
elements.sent-2411-161: Objective experimental results are listed in Table \ref{tab4 .}
elements.sent-2411-162: We can observe that in all metrics, the ESTVocoder outperformed the ESTVocoder-CNXv1, and the Vocos-CNXv2 outperformed the Vocos.
elements.sent-2411-163: This result indicates that ConvNeXt v2 has a stronger modeling capability in vocoder tasks compared to ConvNeXt, which is why we chose ConvNeXt v2 as the backbone for ESTVocoder.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=7cm]{Curve.pdf} \caption{Training curves of mel-spectrogram loss for ESTVocoder and Vocos-CNXv2.} \label{analysis2}
elements.subsubsection-2411-006: Analysis of the role of the excitation producer.
elements.sent-2411-164: As mentioned in Section \ref{sec:excit , the original intention of introducing the excitation spectra in ESTVocoder is to provide the neural filter with prior information about the speech spectra, thereby reducing the training difficulty of the neural filter and improving the quality of the synthesized speech.}
elements.sent-2411-165: To confirm this point, we compared ESTVocoder with Vocos-CNXv2.
elements.sent-2411-166: They both use ConvNeXt v2 as the backbone, so Vocos-CNXv2 can be approximately regarded as the ablated version of ESTVocoder without the excitation producer.
elements.sent-2411-167: We conducted the following three analyses.. 1) Objective evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-168: As shown in Table \ref{tab4 , the ESTVocoder outperformed the Vocos-CNXv2 for all objective metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-169: This confirms that the introduced excitation producer significantly helps improve the quality of synthesized speech.. 2) Visual analysis.
elements.sent-2411-170: Both ESTVocoder and Vocos-CNXv2 used 8 ConvNeXt v2 blocks, so we attempted to reconstruct the speech waveform based on the output from their 4th block (referred to as ESTVocoder* and Vocos-CNXv2*, respectively) to test their performance at intermediate stages of the model.
elements.sent-2411-171: Figure \ref{analysis shows the amplitude spectra of the speeches generated by Vocos-CNXv2*, ESTVocoder*, Vocos-CNXv2, and ESTVocoder.}
elements.sent-2411-172: Interestingly, the amplitude spectrum of ESTVocoder* is close to that of natural speech, while the amplitude spectrum of Vocos-CNXv2* is significantly worse.
elements.sent-2411-173: This indicates that the speech spectral prior introduced by the excitation allows the neural filter to quickly learn the shape of the speech spectra, resulting in a faster learning speed.
elements.sent-2411-174: By comparing Vocos-CNXv2 and ESTVocoder, as shown by the red circles in Figure \ref{analysis , ESTVocoder's harmonic restoration is more accurate, which is also attributed to the introduction of the excitation prior information.}
elements.sent-2411-175: Figure \ref{analysis2 plots the training curves of the mel spectrogram loss for Vocos-CNXv2 and ESTVocoder.}
elements.sent-2411-176: From the figure, we can also see that ESTVocoder's convergence speed is significantly faster, which is also attributed to the excitation prior information provided to the neural filter.
elements.sent-2411-177: In this paper, we present an excitation-spectral-transformed neural vocoder called ESTVocoder under the source-filter framework.
elements.sent-2411-178: An excitation producer produces the excitation waveform according to F0, and then the neural filter transforms the excitation amplitude and phase spectra to speech ones conditioned on the mel spectrogram.
elements.sent-2411-179: The comprehensive experimental results demonstrate that our proposed ESTVocoder outperforms several baseline vocoders.
elements.sent-2411-180: Analysis experiments confirm that the excitation provides the neural filter with speech spectral priors, effectively reducing the training difficulty of the neural filter and improving the quality of synthesized speech.
elements.sent-2411-181: Further improving the efficiency of ESTVocoder and applying it to other tasks (i.e., speech enhancement) will be the focus of our future work.. \bibliographystyle{splncs04_unsort.}
